english: Travel with Mia to Leipzig. Learn vocabulary for history, society, and change. Discover the story of German reunification.
Learn 16 key words about history and society
Join Mia in her hometown Leipzig
Discover the history of German reunification
The ICE stops in Leipzig. Mia is already at the door before the train has stopped. She has been telling Verumius about her city for weeks. Now he is finally here.
This is Leipzig Central Station! One of the largest train stations in Europe. Freshly renovated, with shops inside. It all used to be grey here.
This is a train station? It looks like a palace. In my home we only have small stations. Mia, you could live here.
the border, the boundary
From Old High German greniza, borrowed from Slavic.\nThe most famous German Grenze split the country from 1949 to 1989.

A: Where was the border before?
B: Right through Berlin. Right through Germany.
A: Can you still see the border?
B: In some places yes. There are memorial sites.
Grenzenlos (boundless) became a symbol of reunification. When the Grenze fell, people danced on the wall. The inner-German border was 1,393 km long with fences, mines, and watchtowers.
the wall
From Latin murus. Die Berliner Mauer stood from 1961 to 1989.\nWhen Germans say die Mauer, everyone knows which wall.

A: Did you see the Wall?
B: No, I am too young. But my grandma remembers.
A: What was it like when the Wall fell?
B: She says she cried with joy.
Mauerfall (fall of the wall) needs no further explanation in German. The Berlin Wall fell on November 9, 1989. Pieces of the Wall are now sold as souvenirs worldwide.
freedom
Frei (free) + -heit (abstract suffix).\nThe word that echoed through Nikolaikirche in 1989.

A: What does freedom mean to you?
B: Being able to say what I think.
A: And for the people in the GDR?
B: Being allowed to travel. Being allowed to vote.
Freiheit was the single most powerful word of the peaceful revolution. Leipzig calls itself Heldenstadt (hero city) because of it. 70,000 people marched for Freiheit with candles in October 1989.
society
From Geselle (companion) + -schaft.\nLiterally: companionship. Covers social structures and business.

A: What is a Gesellschaft?
B: All people who live together.
A: And a GmbH?
B: That is a company with limited liability. A business.
A GmbH (Gesellschaft mit beschränkter Haftung) is a company with limited liability. The word covers both grand social structures and small business partnerships. Over 500,000 GmbHs exist in Germany.
historical
From Greek historikos. Germans distinguish historisch\n(historically significant) from geschichtlich (relating to history as a discipline).

A: Is Leipzig a historic city?
B: Yes, very. Bach worked here.
A: And the Nikolaikirche?
B: Historic! The peaceful revolution began there.
Leipzig is historisch because history happened here: Bach composed, Goethe wrote Faust at Auerbachs Keller, and the 1989 peaceful revolution started at Nikolaikirche.
political
From Greek politikos (of citizens).\nPolitische Bildung (political education) is taught in every German school.

A: Is that a political question?
B: Yes, it is about reunification.
A: Was Leipzig politically important?
B: Very. The Monday demonstrations began here.
Politisch korrekt entered German from English. Germans debate politics with passion. Politische Bildung (political education) is a mandatory school subject designed to prevent history from repeating.
national
German national identity is complicated. After 1945, national\nbecame a loaded word. It coexists carefully with European identity.

A: Is there a German national feeling?
B: Yes, but it is complicated after history.
A: When do Germans show national pride?
B: Mostly at the football World Cup. Then it is okay.

Nationalmannschaft (national team) is one of the few safe contexts for national in German. After 1945, the word carried so much weight that Germans developed a careful, reflective relationship with national identity.
the memory, the remembrance
Er- + inner (inner) + -ung. To bring something inward again.\nErinnerungskultur (remembrance culture) is a core German value.

A: Do you have memories of the GDR?
B: No, I am too young. But my family remembers.
A: Are the memories good or bad?
B: Both. It was complicated.
Erinnerungskultur (remembrance culture) defines how Germany confronts its past through memorials, museums, and education. Germany has over 1,200 Holocaust memorials. Remembering is not optional.
the past
From vergangen (gone by). Ver- + gangen (gone).\nThe past that has gone away.

A: The past is important.
B: Yes, one must learn from it.
A: Do Germans often talk about the past?
B: Yes, especially about the time after 1945.

Vergangenheitsbewältigung (coming to terms with the past) is a famously long German word for a famously difficult process. It describes how a society processes and comes to terms with its own history.
the development
From entwickeln (to develop). Ent- (out of) + wickeln (to wrap).\nLiterally: unwrapping. Development is an unwrapping of potential.

A: How was the development after 1989?
B: Fast, but not always easy.
A: What developed?
B: The economy, the infrastructure, the whole society.

Entwicklungsland (developing country) uses the same logic: a land that is unwrapping its potential. After reunification, East Germany went through the largest Entwicklung in European peacetime history.
to arise, to come into being
Ent- (out of) + stehen (to stand). Something standing\nforth into existence. Perfekt: ist entstanden.

A: When did Leipzig come into being?
B: In the 12th century as a trading city.
A: And the university?
B: It was founded in 1409. One of the oldest in Germany.

'Die Stadt ist im 12. Jahrhundert entstanden' shows how Germans describe a city's birth. Entstehen implies organic growth rather than deliberate founding. Cities entstehen. Companies werden gegründet (are founded).
to experience
Er- (achieving prefix) + leben (to live). To truly live\nthrough something. Stronger than Erfahrung (experience gained over time).

A: What did you experience in Leipzig?
B: I visited the Nikolaikirche. That was very moving.
A: Would you like to experience more?
B: Yes! Mia wants to show me a lot more.
Ein Erlebnis (an experience) is something you actively lived through. It is stronger than Erfahrung (experience gained gradually). Germans value Erlebnisse: the moments that stay with you forever.
to find out, to experience
Er- + fahren (to drive/travel). Literally: to travel toward\nknowledge. Also an adjective: ein erfahrener Arzt (an experienced doctor).

A: Did you find out about the history of the Nikolaikirche?
B: Yes, Mia told me everything.
A: How did you find out about it?
B: She told me about it on the train ride.

Erfahren works as both verb and adjective. As a verb: Ich habe erfahren, dass... (I found out that...). As an adjective: ein erfahrener Arzt (an experienced doctor). Travel (fahren) leads to knowledge.
at all, in general
One of German's most versatile words. Adds emphasis,\ndoubt, or generalization depending on context.

A: Do you understand at all what happened here?
B: Not entirely. Tell me more.
A: In general, Leipzig is a special city.
B: I can tell. It has so much history.
Hast du überhaupt verstanden? (Did you understand at all?) adds emphasis. Überhaupt nicht (not at all) is a strong negation. The word shifts meaning with context: emphasis, doubt, or generalization.
above all, especially
Literally: before everything. A connector that signals\nwhat matters most in a statement.

A: What do you like about Leipzig?
B: Above all the history. And the architecture.
A: And the food?
B: Yes, that too. But above all the atmosphere.
Leipzig ist schön, aber vor allem ist es historisch. Vor allem marks what matters most. Germans use it to prioritize in conversation: the thing after vor allem is the real point.
original(ly)
From Ursprung (origin). Ur- (primal/original) + Sprung (leap).\nThe primal leap, the very beginning.

A: What was Leipzig originally?
B: A trading city. Since the Middle Ages.
A: And today?
B: Still. But above all a cultural city.

Ursprünglich wollte ich... (Originally I wanted to...) is how Germans preface changed plans. The Ur- prefix means primal or original. Urlaub (vacation) literally means primal-leave: the original permission to be away.
Verumius stands in the grand hall and looks up. A train station that survived wars and division. Mia smiles. For her, this is all home. For him, it is the beginning of a story.
In 1989, this is what demonstrators in Leipzig were marching for. Think about the opposite of oppression.
This structure divided a city for 28 years. Its fall in 1989 changed history.
The Berlin Wall fell on November 9, 1989.
The peaceful demonstrations that began in Leipzig in 1989 changed German history.
Learn vocabulary for politics, civic life, and social structures. Build the words you need to discuss history and current events in German.
Learn 18 words about politics and society
Discuss civic participation in German
Understand news vocabulary
I want to show you something, Verumius. Not the beautiful buildings. The words. The words my grandparents used to describe their lives. Listen carefully.
the population
From Volk (people). Be- + völker + -ung.\nThe process of populating, and the population itself.

A: How large is the population of Germany?
B: About 84 million.
A: And Leipzig's?
B: About 600,000. The city is growing.
Die Bevölkerung Deutschlands is about 84 million. One word for both the people and the act of peopling a land. After reunification, the Bevölkerung of the former East shrank as millions moved west.
the citizen (male)
From Burg (castle/fortress). Originally the people who lived\ninside the castle walls. Bürgerrecht = civil right.

A: What is a citizen?
B: Someone with rights and duties in a state.
A: The citizens of Leipzig were brave.
B: Yes, they fought for their rights in 1989.
Bürgermeister (mayor) literally means citizen-master. English burgess is a cousin. The 1989 Leipzig demonstrators chanted Wir sind das Volk! (We are the people!), claiming their rights as Bürger.
the citizen (female)
The -in suffix marks the feminine form.\nBürgerinnen und Bürger is the standard inclusive phrasing.

A: Is she a Leipzig citizen?
B: Yes, she was born and raised here.
A: What does the city mean to her?
B: Everything. She is a proud Leipzigerin.
Bürgerinnen und Bürger is the standard inclusive phrasing in official German. The -in ending is mandatory in formal contexts for all professional and civic titles.
violence, power
From walten (to rule). One word, two meanings:\nGewalt anwenden = to use violence. Gewaltenteilung = separation of powers.

A: Was there violence at the demonstrations?
B: No! That is what makes it special. No violence.
A: How is that possible?
B: The people only had candles. No weapons.

The Leipzig revolution was remarkable because it succeeded ohne Gewalt (without violence). Gewalt means both violence and power. Gewaltenteilung (separation of powers) is the foundation of the German constitution.
the war
From Old High German chreg (stubbornness/effort).\nDer Kalte Krieg (the Cold War) divided Germany.

A: What was the Cold War?
B: A conflict between East and West. Without direct fighting.
A: How long did it last?
B: About 45 years. From 1945 to 1990.
Nachkriegszeit (post-war period) shaped everything about modern Germany. A short, heavy word. Krieg carries the weight of two World Wars and the Cold War that divided Germany for 40 years.
peace
Also spelled Frieden (both forms are correct).\nFrom Proto-Germanic frithuz (protection).

A: What does peace mean for Leipzig?
B: Everything. The peace prayers at the Nikolaikirche started everything.
A: Peace prayers?
B: Yes, prayers for peace. Every Monday.
Friedensgebet (peace prayer) was what started the 1989 movement at Nikolaikirche. Friedlich (peaceful) describes how Leipzig changed the world. Friede and Frieden are both correct forms.
the fight, the struggle
From Latin campus (battlefield). Not always physical:\nKampf gegen den Klimawandel (fight against climate change).

A: Was it a fight?
B: Yes, a fight for freedom. But without violence.
A: How do you fight without violence?
B: With words, with candles, with courage.

Kampf um Freiheit (fight for freedom) is one of the most emotionally charged phrases in German. The word serves both physical and abstract struggles. Leipzig's Kampf was won with candles, not weapons.
the reform
From Latin reformare (to reshape).\nReformen durchführen = to carry out reforms.

A: What did the people want in 1989?
B: Reforms. More freedom, free elections.
A: Did they want to abolish the GDR?
B: Not at first. At first they only wanted reforms.

The 1989 movement started as a call for Reform, not reunification. History took its own course. Reformen durchführen (to carry out reforms) is what politicians promise and what the people of Leipzig demanded.
the election, the choice
From wählen (to choose). Freie Wahlen was a key demand\nof the 1989 demonstrators.

A: What does Wahl mean?
B: A decision. Or a political vote.
A: The first free election in the GDR?
B: March 1990. A historic moment.

Freie Wahlen (free elections) was a key demand of the 1989 demonstrators. Die Qual der Wahl (the agony of choice) is a German rhyming idiom for having too many options. Voter turnout in Germany regularly exceeds 70%.
politics
From Greek politike (civic affairs). No plural form.\nIn die Politik gehen = to go into politics.

A: Are you interested in politics?
B: Yes, since I was in Leipzig. The history is fascinating.
A: Politics is important in Germany.
B: Yes, voter turnout is very high.
Germans take their Politik seriously: voter turnout regularly exceeds 70%. In die Politik gehen (to go into politics) is a respected choice. Political education is mandatory in German schools.
the politician (male)
Politik + -er. Ein erfahrener Politiker uses adjective\ndeclension you will learn this unit.

A: Who was an important politician of reunification?
B: Helmut Kohl. He was Federal Chancellor.
A: And from Leipzig?
B: Many civil rights activists later became politicians.
German political titles are long: Bundestagsabgeordneter (member of the Bundestag) has 24 letters. Ein erfahrener Politiker uses the adjective declension pattern you will learn in this unit.
the politician (female)
The -in suffix is standard, not optional, in formal German\nfor all professional titles.

A: Are there many female politicians in Germany?
B: Yes, more and more. Germany had a female Chancellor.
A: Who was that?
B: Angela Merkel. She governed for 16 years.
Germany has had a female Chancellor (Bundeskanzlerin) and numerous female ministers. The -in ending is standard, not optional, in formal German for all professional titles.
the pastor, the reverend
Masculine noun. From Latin 'parochus' (parish priest).\nFeminine: die Pfarrerin.
A: Who is the pastor here?\nB: The pastor of the Nikolaikirche.

In Leipzig 1989, Pastor Christian Führer led the Nikolaikirche's Monday peace prayers (Friedensgebete) for years. These gatherings grew into the mass demonstrations that helped end the GDR peacefully.
the speech
From reden (to speak/talk). Eine Rede halten = to give a speech.\nRedefreiheit = freedom of speech.

A: Did someone give a speech?
B: Yes, the pastor at the Nikolaikirche.
A: About what?
B: About peace and nonviolence.

Wovon ist die Rede? (What are we talking about?) is everyday German. Redefreiheit (freedom of speech) was one of the rights the 1989 demonstrators fought for. A Rede is both a formal speech and any topic of conversation.
the public, publicity
From öffentlich (public). Offen (open) + -lich + -keit.\nTriple suffix chain. The space where citizens and government meet.

A: Was the revolution public?
B: Yes, it took place in public.
A: Could everyone watch?
B: Yes. The whole world was watching.

In der Öffentlichkeit (in public) is where citizens and government meet. Die Öffentlichkeit informieren (to inform the public) was impossible in the DDR. The 1989 revolution brought everything into the Öffentlichkeit.
public
From offen (open). Öffentliche Verkehrsmittel = public transport.\nÖffentliche Meinung = public opinion.

A: Is that a public event?
B: Yes, everyone can come.
A: Is there public transport?
B: Yes, the tram goes directly there.
What is öffentlich belongs to everyone. Contrast: privat (private). Öffentliche Verkehrsmittel (public transport) and öffentliche Meinung (public opinion) are everyday German phrases.
social
From Latin socialis. Soziale Marktwirtschaft is Germany's\neconomic model: capitalism with a social safety net.

A: What does sozial mean?
B: It is about how people live together.
A: And social market economy?
B: Free market with social protection. The German model.

Soziale Marktwirtschaft (social market economy) is Germany's economic model: capitalism with a social safety net. Sozialwohnung (social housing) and Sozialhilfe (social welfare) are core institutions.
to lead, to guide
From Proto-Germanic forjan. Führung = leadership/guided tour.\nEin Gespräch führen = to lead a conversation.

A: Who led the peace prayers?
B: The pastor of the Nikolaikirche.
A: He led the people?
B: Yes, with words. Not with violence.

Führung means both leadership and guided tour. Ein Gespräch führen (to lead a conversation) and Krieg führen (to wage war) use the same verb. The word serves both peaceful guidance and forceful direction.
to serve
From Proto-Germanic thewjan. Dem Frieden dienen = to serve peace.\nDer Dienst = service.

A: What did the Nikolaikirche serve?
B: Peace. It was a place of prayer.
A: And today?
B: It serves remembrance. It is a monument of peace.
Beamter im öffentlichen Dienst (civil servant) is a formal title. In Leipzig, many felt they had served a system that did not serve them back. Dienen implies duty and dedication.
My grandparents lived in the GDR. They did not trust each other right away. That was normal. You never knew who worked for the Stasi.
So many words for things I have never experienced. Border, violence, struggle. And then: freedom, peace, hope. The language carries the whole history.
In 1989, people demanded the right to choose their leaders. What do we call this political process?
The citizens marched and risked arrest to demand this. It is what authoritarian systems take away.
The citizens demanded freedom and democracy.
What is offen (open) belongs to everyone and is visible to all.
Explore Leipzig's historic center with Mia. Learn adjective declension without articles (strong endings). Discuss monuments, influence, and cultural change.
Learn 16 words about monuments and cultural change
Master adjective declension without articles
Form opinions about history with einerseits/andererseits
This is the market square. And there: the Old Town Hall. Renaissance architecture from the 16th century. Goethe sat in Auerbachs Keller and wrote Faust. Real historic ground!
They go down into the cellar. Dark wood, old paintings on the walls, the smell of centuries. Verumius touches the wall. Goethe sat here. This is not a museum. This is a living place.
the monument, the memorial
Denk (think) + Mal (mark/time). A thinking-mark.\nDenkmalschutz (monument protection) is serious in Germany.

A: What is this monument?
B: A monument to the peaceful revolution.
A: Are there many monuments in Leipzig?
B: Yes, everywhere. Leipzig does not forget.
A Denkmal is a thinking-mark, a place that makes you think. Denkmalschutz (monument protection) is serious in Germany: you cannot even change the windows of a protected building without permission.
the influence
Ein (in) + Fluss (flow). Something flowing in.\nGroßen Einfluss haben = to have great influence.

A: Did Leipzig have influence on music?
B: Enormous influence! Bach, Mendelssohn, Schumann.
A: And on politics?
B: Also. The Monday demonstrations changed everything.
Leipzig's influence on German music is enormous: Bach, Mendelssohn, and Schumann all worked here. The Gewandhaus Orchestra is one of the oldest in the world, founded in 1743.
progress
Fort (forward) + Schritt (step). A forward step.\nFortschritte machen = to make progress.

A: Is there progress in East Germany?
B: Yes, but slowly. It takes time.
A: What kind of progress?
B: Economic, social, and in infrastructure.

The word Fortschritt itself walks forward: Fort (forward) + Schritt (step). Technischer Fortschritt (technological progress) is a favorite German compound. After reunification, Fortschritt in the east was rapid but uneven.
the generation
From Latin generatio. Die nächste Generation = the next generation.\nGermans speak of Kriegsgeneration and Nachkriegsgeneration.

A: Does the young generation understand the GDR?
B: Not directly. But it learns from it.
A: And the older generation?
B: They experienced it. They remember.
Germans speak of the Kriegsgeneration, Nachkriegsgeneration, and Wiedervereinigungsgeneration. Each carries different weight. The generation gap between Ost and West still shapes German society.
the symbol
From Greek symbolon (sign/token).\nLeipzigs Nikolaikirche ist ein Symbol der friedlichen Revolution.

A: What is a symbol of unity?
B: The Brandenburg Gate. Everyone knows it.
A: And for Leipzig?
B: The Nikolaikirche. A symbol of peaceful change.

Das Brandenburger Tor ist ein Symbol der Einheit (The Brandenburg Gate is a symbol of unity). Leipzig's Nikolaikirche is a Symbol of the peaceful revolution. Symbols carry deeper meaning than mere signs.
the sign, the mark
From zeichnen (to draw). Ein Zeichen setzen = to make a statement.\nMore concrete than Symbol. Traffic signs are Zeichen.

A: The candles were a sign.
B: Yes, a sign for peace.
A: What kind of sign?
B: A sign that violence is not necessary.
Ein Zeichen setzen (to set a sign, to make a statement) is what the people of Leipzig did. More concrete than Symbol: a Zeichen is something visible, a Symbol carries deeper meaning.
the effect, the impact
From wirken (to have an effect). Nebenwirkung = side effect.\nLangfristige Wirkung = long-term effect.

A: What effect did the demonstrations have?
B: An enormous effect. The Wall fell.
A: Does Leipzig still have this effect today?
B: Yes, as a reminder of what is possible.

Nebenwirkung (side effect) is what you read on medicine packages. Langfristige Wirkung (long-term effect) is how historians describe what happened in Leipzig. The Wirkung of the peaceful revolution is still felt today.
hope
From hoffen (to hope). Die Hoffnung stirbt zuletzt = hope dies last.\nIn 1989, Hoffnung brought 70,000 people onto the streets.

A: Did the people have hope?
B: Yes, despite everything. They hoped for change.
A: And today?
B: Hope is still there. Leipzig's motto.
Die Hoffnung stirbt zuletzt (hope dies last) is a German proverb. In 1989, Hoffnung was the fuel that brought 70,000 people onto Leipzig's streets with candles. Hope, not weapons, changed history.
critical
From Greek kritikos (able to judge). Kritisch denken = to think critically.\nBeing kritisch means you think carefully, not negatively.

A: Is it important to think critically?
B: Yes, especially with historical topics.
A: Why?
B: Because you have to see different perspectives.

Germans value kritisches Denken (critical thinking) in education. Being kritisch is not negative: it means you think carefully and question assumptions. Eine kritische Masse (a critical mass) uses the scientific meaning.
frequent(ly)
From Haufen (pile/heap). Something that piles up happens häufig.\nMore formal than oft (often) but means the same.

A: Do tourists come to Leipzig frequently?
B: Yes, more and more often. The city is becoming more popular.
A: What do they visit most frequently?
B: The Nikolaikirche and Auerbachs Keller.
Häufig gestellte Fragen (frequently asked questions, FAQ) uses this word. Häufig comes from Haufen (pile/heap): something that piles up happens often.
constant(ly)
From Stand (standing/state). Something that keeps standing.\nOften used with mild complaint: Sie redet ständig.

A: Is Leipzig constantly changing?
B: Yes, constantly. The city continues to develop.
A: Is that good?
B: Mostly yes. But some miss the old ways.
Ständige Vertretung (permanent representation) is a diplomatic term. In everyday German, ständig often carries mild complaint: Sie redet ständig (She talks constantly). Implies something unending.
eternal
From Old High German ewig (age-long).\nEwig und drei Tage = forever and three days (German idiom).

A: Does the renovation take forever?
B: Almost! They have been building for three years.
A: Is Leipzig always under construction?
B: No, but sometimes it feels that way.

Ewig und drei Tage (forever and three days) is the German version of forever and a day. Das Ewige Licht (the eternal light) burns in every Catholic church. Ewig carries both sacred and everyday meanings.
to change, to alter
Ver- + ändern (to make different). Reflexive: sich verändern.\nStronger than ändern: implies transformation.

A: Has Leipzig changed?
B: Totally. After the Wende everything changed.
A: For the better?
B: Mostly yes. The city has become more vibrant.

Die Welt verändern (to change the world) is what the people of Leipzig did. Verändern implies transformation, deeper than just ändern. Sich verändern (to change oneself) is reflexive: the change comes from within.
to reflect, to think about
Nach (after) + denken (to think). Thinking-after, pondering.\nSeparable: Ich denke darüber nach. Requires über + accusative.

A: Do you think about the history?
B: Yes, constantly. Leipzig makes you thoughtful.
A: What do you think about?
B: About what freedom really means.
Denk mal darüber nach (Think about it) is an invitation to reflect. Nachdenken is separable: Ich denke darüber nach. The nach- gives denken depth: not just thinking, but thinking-after.
to have an effect, to seem
Das wirkt komisch (That seems strange). Er wirkt müde (He seems tired).\nBridges appearance and reality.

A: How does Leipzig seem to you?
B: Impressive. The city seems vibrant and historic.
A: And the Nikolaikirche?
B: It seems peaceful. You feel the history.

Wirken bridges appearance and reality: Das wirkt komisch (That seems strange) describes how something appears. An etwas wirken (to work on something) describes real action. One verb, two perspectives.
on one hand
Einer (one) + seits (side). Always paired with andererseits.\nA connector for balanced arguments.

A: What do you think about reunification?
B: On one hand it was wonderful. Freedom for everyone.
A: And on the other hand?
B: On the other hand it was hard. A lot had to change.
Einerseits ist Leipzig schön, andererseits hat es eine schwere Geschichte. This connector pair is essential for B1 argumentation: presenting balanced views on complex topics.

When no article stands before the adjective, the adjective takes over the article's job. It shows gender, number, and case.

Nominative:
- masculine: guter Wein (like der)
- feminine: kalte Milch (like die)
- neuter: frisches Brot (like das)
- plural: neue Ideen (like die)

Accusative:
- masculine: guten Wein (like den)
- feminine: kalte Milch (like die)
- neuter: frisches Brot (like das)
- plural: neue Ideen (like die)

Dative:
- masculine: gutem Wein (like dem)
- feminine: kalter Milch (like der)
- neuter: frischem Brot (like dem)
- plural: neuen Ideen (like den)

Remember: The adjective does the article's job.

Adjectives without articles commonly appear with:

1. Mass nouns: kaltes Wasser, frische Milch
2. Abstract nouns: politische Freiheit, soziale Gerechtigkeit
3. Plurals without articles: neue Ideen, alte Freunde
4. After numbers: zwei gute Bücher
5. In lists: mit großem Mut und starker Hoffnung

The endings match the endings of the definite article (der/die/das/den/dem).
The adjective takes the ending of the definite article. It does the article's job.
On one hand I find the architecture beautiful. On the other hand I feel the past everywhere here. Every stone has experienced something. Every building tells a story.
Neutral Nominativ without article needs the ending -es, like das.
Geschichte is feminine. Feminine Nominativ without article takes -e.
Leipzig has a long history.
Masculine Akkusativ without article takes -en, matching den.
Tee is masculine Akkusativ (-en). Brot is neuter Akkusativ (-es). Butter is feminine Dativ (-er).
Verumius drinks hot tea and eats fresh bread with good butter.
Stand in the church where the peaceful revolution of 1989 began. Learn adjective declension in the genitive. Learn reciprocal verbs. Express emotional reactions to history.
Master adjective declension in the genitive (always -en)
Learn reciprocal verbs (sich, einander, miteinander)
Express emotional reactions to history
The Nikolaikirche is quiet. Pink-white columns rise like palm trees to the ceiling. Everything began here. Here, people prayed for freedom while armed police waited outside.
In October 1989, 70,000 people came here. Without violence. Only with candles. The police were there, but nobody fired. That was the beginning of the end.
I sit quietly in the church pew. The palm columns reach to the ceiling. I imagine 70,000 people walking outside with candles. My heart beats faster. This is sacred ground.
the assembly, the gathering
Ver- + sammeln (to collect) + -ung. A coming-together.\nVersammlungsfreiheit = freedom of assembly.

A: Were the Monday demonstrations assemblies?
B: Yes, peaceful assemblies. Every Monday.
A: Was that allowed?
B: No. But the people came anyway.
Versammlungsfreiheit (freedom of assembly) is a constitutional right in Germany. The 1989 Montagsdemonstrationen were Versammlungen that changed history: illegal under DDR law, but unstoppable.
the community
Gemein (common) + -schaft. What people share in common.\nWarmer than Gesellschaft: built on bonds, not structures.

A: What is the difference between Gesellschaft and Gemeinschaft?
B: Gesellschaft is the structure. Gemeinschaft is the feeling.
A: Was there community in the GDR?
B: Yes, despite everything. The neighbors helped each other.
Europäische Gemeinschaft (European Community) was the predecessor of the EU. Gemeinschaft is warmer and more personal than Gesellschaft (society): built on bonds rather than structures.
youth
No plural form. Die Jugend refers to young people collectively.\nJugendherberge (youth hostel) is a German invention.

A: What does the youth in Leipzig do?
B: A lot of culture. Music, art, theater.
A: Is the youth interested in history?
B: Yes, more and more. The schools teach it well.
In meiner Jugend... (In my youth...) is how stories of the DDR often begin. Jugendherberge (youth hostel) is a German invention. Die Jugend has no plural: it is always collective.
the witness (male)
From zeugen (to testify). Weak noun: der Zeuge, den Zeugen.\nZeitzeuge = contemporary witness who lived through history.

A: Are there still contemporary witnesses of 1989?
B: Yes, many. Mia's grandparents for example.
A: What do the contemporary witnesses tell?
B: About fear, hope, and change.

Zeitzeuge (contemporary witness) is someone who lived through a historical period. Leipzig has many Zeitzeugen of 1989. Their stories carry the weight of lived experience. The weak noun declension (den Zeugen, dem Zeugen) is characteristic.
the witness (female)
The -in suffix marks the feminine form.\nAls Zeugin aussagen = to testify as a witness.

A: Is your grandma a contemporary witness?
B: Yes, she experienced the Wende.
A: What does she tell?
B: That she never believed the Wall would fall.
Mia's grandparents are Zeitzeuginnen and Zeitzeugen of the DDR era. Their stories carry the weight of lived experience. In Germany, Zeitzeugen regularly visit schools to share their memories.
reality
From Latin realitas. Die Realität sieht anders aus = reality looks different.\nBorrowed word, paired with native Wirklichkeit.

A: Was the GDR a good reality?
B: For some yes. For many no.
A: What did reality look like?
B: Restricted freedom. Surveillance. But also community.

Germans sometimes pair Realität with Wirklichkeit for emphasis: both mean reality, but Realität is borrowed from Latin, Wirklichkeit is native German. Die Realität sieht anders aus (Reality looks different) is everyday German.
reality
From wirklich (real). Wirk- (effect/work) + -lich + -keit.\nWhat actually has effect, what truly works.

A: What is the difference between Realität and Wirklichkeit?
B: Almost none. Wirklichkeit sounds more German.
A: In reality?
B: In reality they mean the same thing.
In Wirklichkeit (in reality) is how Germans reveal what is really going on beneath the surface. Wirklichkeit comes from wirken (to have an effect): reality is what actually has effect in the world.
simultaneous(ly)
Gleich (same) + Zeit (time) + -ig. At the same time.\nDescribes the feeling in Nikolaikirche perfectly.

A: How does it feel in the Nikolaikirche?
B: Simultaneously sad and hopeful.
A: How is that possible?
B: The beauty and the history coexist.
Gleichzeitig traurig und hoffnungsvoll (simultaneously sad and hopeful) describes the feeling in Nikolaikirche perfectly. The beauty of the columns and the weight of what happened here coexist.
human, humane
From Mensch (human being). Menschliche Würde = human dignity.\nArticle 1 of the German constitution.

A: What is human dignity?
B: The right to be respected as a human being.
A: Is that in the constitution?
B: Yes, Article 1. Human dignity is inviolable.
Menschliche Würde (human dignity) is Article 1 of the German constitution. Menschlich means both human and humane. To be human is to be compassionate. This principle shaped post-war Germany.
completely
From voll (full). Stronger than ganz (quite).\nVöllig unerwartet = completely unexpected.

A: Was the fall of the Wall completely unexpected?
B: Yes, completely! Nobody expected it.
A: Not even the politicians?
B: No, completely surprising for everyone.
Völlig unerwartet (completely unexpected) describes what happened in November 1989. An intensifier stronger than ganz (quite). Völlig adds fullness to any adjective it precedes.
to happen, to occur
Irregular: geschieht, geschah, ist geschehen. More formal than\npassieren. Geschichte (history) comes from the same root.

A: What happened in the Nikolaikirche?
B: The peace prayers began here.
A: When did that happen?
B: From 1982 onward. Every Monday.
Was ist geschehen? (What happened?) carries weight and formality. Geschichte (history) comes from the same root: history is what has happened. Geschehen is reserved for significant events.
to develop
Ent- (out of) + wickeln (to wrap). To unwrap.\nReflexive: sich entwickeln = to develop organically.

A: Did Leipzig develop after the Wende?
B: Yes, enormously. The city completely changed.
A: What developed?
B: Everything. Economy, culture, infrastructure.

Sich entwickeln (to develop oneself) is reflexive for organic growth. Die Stadt hat sich schnell entwickelt (The city developed quickly). Transitive entwickeln is for deliberate creation: eine App entwickeln (to develop an app).
to consider, to think over
Über (over) + legen (to lay). To lay thoughts over a matter.\nAlso an adjective: überlegen = superior.

A: Are you still thinking it over?
B: Yes, I am considering what this church means to me.
A: Take your time.
B: The history here is so heavy. I need to reflect.
Ich überlege mir das (I will think it over) is how Germans buy time for decisions. As an adjective, überlegen means superior: same word, but stress changes meaning.
on the other hand
Anderer (other) + seits (side). Partner of einerseits.\nEssential for B1 argumentation.

A: On one hand, reunification is good.
B: On the other hand, there are still differences.
A: Between East and West?
B: Yes. Economically and sometimes culturally.
Andererseits muss man die Geschichte verstehen (On the other hand, one must understand history). Essential for B1 argumentation. Always paired with einerseits to present balanced views.

After every genitive article (des, der, des, der, eines, einer, eines), the adjective ALWAYS ends in -en. No exceptions.

Examples:
- des guten Mannes (masc)
- der guten Frau (fem)
- des guten Kindes (neut)
- der guten Leute (plural)
- eines guten Mannes
- einer guten Frau
- eines guten Kindes

Common contexts:
- während des Kalten Krieges
- trotz des schlechten Wetters
- wegen des großen Erfolgs

Genitive is the easiest case for adjective declension: always -en with an article.

These prepositions require the genitive:

während = during: während des Krieges
trotz = despite: trotz des Regens
wegen = because of: wegen des Wetters
außerhalb = outside of: außerhalb der Stadt
innerhalb = inside of: innerhalb eines Jahres
statt/anstatt = instead of: statt des Buches

In colloquial speech, some Germans use dative after these prepositions, but in written German the genitive is correct.

When a plural subject does something TO EACH OTHER, German uses sich (ambiguous) or einander (unambiguous).

Sie kennen sich. = They know each other. OR They know themselves.
Sie kennen einander. = Unambiguous: each other.

With prepositions, einander becomes one word:
- miteinander (with each other)
- füreinander (for each other)
- voneinander (from each other)
- zueinander (to each other)

Comparison:
- Sie waschen sich. = reflexive (themselves)
- Sie helfen einander. = reciprocal (each other)

Common reciprocal verbs:

sich kennen / einander kennen = to know each other
sich verstehen / einander verstehen = to understand each other
sich vertrauen / einander vertrauen = to trust each other
sich helfen / einander helfen = to help each other
sich sehen / einander sehen = to see each other

With preposition + einander:
Sie sprechen miteinander. = They speak with each other.
Sie sorgen füreinander. = They care for each other.
Sie lernen voneinander. = They learn from each other.

Einander is more formal and unambiguous than sich.
the dialect
From Greek dialektos (manner of speaking).\nSächsisch is Leipzig's local dialect.

A: Do people speak dialect in Leipzig?
B: Yes, Saxon. But not everyone.
A: Is the dialect hard to understand?
B: Sometimes. But it is also charming.
Germany has dozens of distinct Dialekte. Sächsisch (Saxon) is Leipzig's dialect, often parodied but deeply loved by locals. Dialekt is identity: where you come from lives in how you speak.
They leave the Nikolaikirche in silence. Outside the sun shines on the Nikolaikirchhof. Mia takes Verumius by the arm. Some places change you, she says quietly. You only realize it later.
After des in the Genitiv, adjectives always end in -en.
Which word explicitly means each other and removes ambiguity?
East and West Germans did not understand each other right away.
After der in the feminine Genitiv, the adjective always takes -en.
First: adjective after des. Second: the unambiguous reciprocal pronoun. Third: the compound meaning 'about each other.'
During the Cold War, East and West barely knew each other. They knew little about each other.
Dativ Plural without article: the adjective takes -en, matching den.
Experience Hildi's most human moment. Learn vocabulary for childhood, homeland, and social structures. Practice all three grammar constructions with personal stories.
Learn 18 words about childhood and social structures
Experience Hildi's most vulnerable moment
Practice strong endings, genitive, and reciprocal verbs
It is late. Verumius comes up the stairs. Hildi sits on the bench in the stairwell, a photo album on her lap. She does not look up. Her eyes are moist.
My husband and I met in Leipzig. We trusted each other immediately. That was rare in the GDR. He always said: One day the wall will fall. He was right.
Here. That is Werner. Leipzig, 1971. He stands in front of the Gewandhaus. He loved music. Bach, Mendelssohn, everything. He did not live to see the reunification. He missed it by three years.
childhood
Kind (child) + -heit. The -heit suffix creates abstract nouns.\nMeine Kindheit in der DDR is how millions begin their stories.

A: How was your childhood?
B: Simple. We did not have much, but we were happy.
A: Where did you grow up?
B: In Leipzig. A beautiful childhood, despite everything.

Meine Kindheit in der DDR (my childhood in the GDR) is how millions of Germans begin their stories. The -heit suffix turns concrete words into abstract states: Kind becomes the state of being a child.
homesickness
Heim (home) + Weh (pain/ache). Home-ache.\nFernweh (distance-ache) is its opposite: longing to travel.

A: Do you have homesickness for Leipzig?
B: Sometimes. Especially in autumn.
A: What do you miss?
B: The dialect. The food. The people.
The German word is so precise it was borrowed into other languages. Fernweh (distance-ache) is its opposite: the longing to travel. Germans invented both words for both directions of longing.
the home
Warmer than Haus (house). Heimat (homeland) builds on this root.\nSich heimisch fühlen = to feel at home.

A: What is a Heim?
B: More than a house. A place where you feel safe.
A: Is Leipzig Mia's home?
B: Yes, always. Even though she lives in Berlin.
Altenheim (retirement home), Kinderheim (children's home), Tierheim (animal shelter): Heim is where you belong. Heimat (homeland) builds on this root. Heim is always warmer than Haus.
the flight, the escape
From fliehen (to flee). Fluchtversuch from the DDR was a crime.\nOver 3.5 million fled East Germany before the Wall was built.

A: Were there many escape attempts?
B: Yes, thousands. Many were dangerous.
A: Why was the escape so difficult?
B: Wall, barbed wire, armed border soldiers.
Flüchtling (refugee) comes from Flucht. Over 3.5 million people fled East Germany before the Wall was built. After the Wall went up, Fluchtversuch (escape attempt) became a life-threatening crime.
the experience (lived)
Er- + leben (to live) + -nis. Something you actively lived through.\nStronger than Erfahrung. The -nis ending is always neuter.

A: What was your greatest experience in Leipzig?
B: The Nikolaikirche. An unforgettable experience.
A: Why?
B: You feel the history. It is overwhelming.
Ein unvergessliches Erlebnis (an unforgettable experience) is something you actively lived through. The -nis ending creates neuter nouns: das Erlebnis, das Ergebnis, das Geheimnis.
upbringing, education
From erziehen (to raise). Er- + ziehen (to pull). Pulling someone up.\nGute Erziehung (good upbringing) is highly valued.

A: Was upbringing in the GDR different?
B: Yes, very. The state had a lot of influence.
A: In what way?
B: The children learned what the state wanted.
Erziehungsurlaub (parental leave) shows how seriously Germany takes Erziehung. Gute Erziehung (good upbringing) is a compliment. In the DDR, Erziehung was heavily state-controlled.
the relationship, the ratio
Ver- + halten (to hold) + -nis. How things hold together.\nAlso means affair in romantic context.

A: How is the relationship between East and West today?
B: Better than before. But not yet perfect.
A: What is missing?
B: More understanding for each other.
Das Verhältnis zwischen Ost und West (the relationship between East and West) is still a topic in Germany. Plural Verhältnisse means conditions/circumstances. One word, many layers.
behavior
Ver- + halten (to hold). How one holds oneself.\nAlso a verb: sich verhalten = to behave.

A: How was the behavior of the police in 1989?
B: At first aggressive. Then restrained.
A: Why?
B: They saw that the people were peaceful.
In the DDR, richtiges Verhalten (correct behavior) was state-defined. Auffälliges Verhalten (conspicuous behavior) could lead to surveillance. Sich verhalten (to behave) is reflexive.
gender, sex, lineage
From schlagen (to strike, originally: kind/type).\nAlso means generation/lineage: das Geschlecht der Habsburger.

A: What does Geschlecht mean?
B: Several things: gender like male/female.
A: And in grammar?
B: Also! Masculine, feminine, neuter: the grammatical gender.

Geschlechtergleichheit (gender equality) is a core value. Das Geschlecht der Habsburger (the House of Habsburg) uses the lineage meaning. Grammar uses it too: grammatisches Geschlecht = grammatical gender.
the majority
Mehr (more) + -heit. The-more-ness.\nZweidrittelmehrheit = two-thirds majority for constitutional changes.

A: Did the majority want reunification?
B: Yes, the vast majority.
A: Right away?
B: In March 1990, at the first free election: yes.
Die Mehrheit der Bevölkerung (the majority of the population) voted for reunification. Zweidrittelmehrheit (two-thirds majority) is needed for constitutional changes. Opposite: Minderheit.
the minority
Minder (less) + -heit. The-less-ness.\nGermany recognizes four national minorities with constitutional protection.

A: Are there minorities in Germany?
B: Yes, for example Danes, Frisians, Sorbs, and Sinti/Roma.
A: Do they have special rights?
B: Yes, they are under special protection.
Germany recognizes four national minorities: Danes, Frisians, Sorbs, and Sinti/Roma. Each has constitutional protection. Ethnische Minderheiten (ethnic minorities) have specific rights in German law.
religion
From Latin religio. Germany has a unique system: Kirchensteuer\n(church tax) is automatically deducted from your salary.

A: Does religion play a role in Germany?
B: Less than before. But the church tax still exists.
A: What is church tax?
B: A tax that is automatically deducted if you belong to a church.

Germany's Kirchensteuer (church tax) is automatically deducted from your salary unless you formally leave your church. Religionsfreiheit (religious freedom) is constitutional. In East Germany under the DDR, religion was suppressed.
the trade union
Ge- + Werk (work) + -schaft. A collective of workers.\nIG Metall is Germany's largest with 2+ million members.

A: What is a trade union?
B: An organization that fights for workers' rights.
A: Are trade unions strong in Germany?
B: Yes, very. They negotiate wages for entire industries.

IG Metall is Germany's largest Gewerkschaft with over 2 million members. Unions are powerful in Germany: they negotiate industry-wide wages. In the DDR, unions existed but were controlled by the state.
equal (in rights)
Gleich (equal) + berechtigt (entitled).\nArtikel 3: Männer und Frauen sind gleichberechtigt.

A: Are men and women equal in rights in Germany?
B: According to the constitution yes. In practice not quite yet.
A: What is missing?
B: Equal pay. Equal opportunities in leadership positions.
Article 3 of the German constitution: Männer und Frauen sind gleichberechtigt (Men and women have equal rights). Four syllables, one fundamental principle. Reality still catches up to the law.
constant(ly), lasting
From dauern (to last). More emotional than ständig:\nimplies slight annoyance or endlessness.

A: Does it rain constantly in Leipzig?
B: Not constantly. But frequently in autumn.
A: That is annoying.
B: Yes, it rains constantly when you do not have an umbrella.
Das dauert dauernd! (That always takes forever!) uses the word playfully. Dauernd is more emotional than ständig: it implies slight annoyance or a feeling of endlessness.
so-called
So (so) + genannt (called, from nennen). Can be neutral\nor skeptical. Sogenannte Experten implies doubt.

A: What was the so-called Wende?
B: The peaceful revolution and reunification of 1989/90.
A: Why so-called?
B: Wende is the informal name. Officially: Wiedervereinigung.

Die sogenannte friedliche Revolution (the so-called peaceful revolution) uses sogenannt neutrally as a naming device. But sogenannte Experten (so-called experts) implies they are not really experts. Context determines tone.
to flee
Irregular: flieht, floh, ist geflohen.\nAus der DDR fliehen was life-threatening.

A: Did many people flee from the GDR?
B: Yes, millions, before the Wall was built.
A: And after that?
B: Also. But it was extremely dangerous.
Over 5,000 people successfully fled across the Berlin Wall. Flüchtig (fleeting, fugitive) comes from this verb. Aus der DDR fliehen was one of the most dangerous decisions a person could make.
to raise, to educate
Irregular: erzieht, erzog, hat erzogen. Er- + ziehen (to pull).\nGut erzogen (well-raised) is a compliment.

A: Who raised you?
B: My grandparents. My parents worked a lot.
A: How did they raise you?
B: Strictly, but lovingly. Well-raised, say the neighbors.
Kinder erziehen (to raise children) uses the same verb. Der Erzieher/die Erzieherin works in kindergartens. In the DDR, the state played a large role in Erziehung.
Hildi closes the album. Her hands tremble slightly. Verumius says nothing. Some moments need no words. He sits down beside her, and they sit together in the silence of the stairwell.
Einander is the explicitly reciprocal word, with no ambiguity.
After der in the Genitiv Plural, adjectives end in -en. Always.
During the long years in the GDR, Hildi experienced a lot.
First: adjective ending after trotz with a feminine noun and no article. Second: the unambiguous reciprocal pronoun. Third: adjective for masculine Akkusativ without article.
Despite great fear, the neighbors helped each other. They showed real courage.
Experience the Sie-to-du switch as an emotional milestone. Learn vocabulary for institutions, discovery, and pressure. Practice all three grammar constructions in combination.
Experience the Sie-to-du switch as a sign of trust
Learn 16 new words about institutions and discovery
Combine all three grammar constructions
Verumius... we have known each other for almost a year now. We have helped each other through difficult times. You can go ahead and say du.
That... that means a lot to me, Hildi. Thank you. I mean... thank you for trusting me.
Werner would have liked you, Verumius. He liked people who ask questions. Come. I will make us tea. And then I will tell you about Leipzig. About our Leipzig.
The next day, Mia and Verumius go to a Leipzig restaurant. Mia orders in Saxon dialect. The waiter answers in Saxon dialect. Verumius understands about half.
the point of view
Stand (standing) + Punkt (point). The point where you stand.\nMeinen Standpunkt vertreten = to represent my point of view.

A: What is your point of view on reunification?
B: On one hand it was necessary. On the other hand it was hard.
A: That is a nuanced point of view.
B: Yes, there are no simple answers here.
Your Standpunkt is the ground you stand on. More concrete than Meinung (opinion). Meinen Standpunkt vertreten (to represent my point of view) implies firmness and conviction.
the introduction
Ein (in) + Führung (leading). Leading into something.\nAlso: the introduction section of a book.

A: What was the introduction of the D-Mark in the East?
B: An important step. In July 1990.
A: Was that good?
B: On one hand yes. On the other hand many businesses went bankrupt.
Die Einführung des Euro (the introduction of the Euro) changed everyday life. The word works for introducing systems, currencies, or ideas. Einführungskurs (introductory course) uses the same root.
the invention
From erfinden (to invent). Er- + finden (to find).\nFinding something into existence.

A: What are famous German inventions?
B: The printing press, aspirin, the MP3 format.
A: The MP3 format?
B: Yes, from the Fraunhofer Institute. A German invention.
Deutsche Erfindungen include the printing press (Gutenberg), aspirin (Bayer), and the MP3 format (Fraunhofer). Er- + finden: to find something into existence. Every invention is a finding.
activity, occupation
From tätig (active). Berufliche Tätigkeit = professional activity.\nMore formal than Arbeit (work).

A: What is your occupation?
B: I work as a social worker.
A: Is that a voluntary activity?
B: No, that is my profession. I volunteer at the animal shelter.
Berufliche Tätigkeit (professional activity) is what official forms ask for. Ehrenamtliche Tätigkeit (volunteer work) is highly valued in Germany. Over 30 million Germans volunteer regularly.
representation, substitute
From vertreten (to represent). Die Vertretung der Bürger\nis what parliament does.

A: Who is the substitute for the boss?
B: Today I am the substitute.
A: And in parliament?
B: Parliament is the representation of the people.
Die Vertretung der Bürger (representation of citizens) is what parliament does. Also: Ich bin die Vertretung (I am the substitute) when someone is absent from work. One word, two contexts.
the representative (male)
From vertreten. Handelsvertreter = sales representative.\nVolksvertreter = people's representative (politician).

A: Who represents the citizens?
B: The delegates. They are representatives of the people.
A: And in the GDR?
B: There were also people's representatives. But without free elections.
In the DDR, Volksvertreter (people's representatives) was an ironic term since the people could not choose them. Today, Vertreter implies genuine representation.
the representative (female)
The -in suffix marks the feminine form.\nUsed in all formal contexts.

A: Who is the union representative?
B: Mrs. Müller. She negotiates for the workers.
A: Is she a good representative?
B: Yes, she fights for fair wages.
Modern German increasingly uses both forms in official documents: Vertreterinnen und Vertreter. Die Vertreterin der Gewerkschaft (the union representative) negotiates for workers' rights.
pressure, print
Two meanings, one word. Unter Druck stehen = to be under pressure.\nDruckerei = printing house. Gutenberg: der Buchdruck.

A: Were the people in the GDR under pressure?
B: Yes, enormous pressure. Politically and personally.
A: And what else does Druck mean?
B: Printing! Gutenberg invented book printing.
Unter Druck stehen (to be under pressure) and Buchdruck (book printing) use the same word. Gutenberg's invention was der Buchdruck. Context separates the meanings clearly.
gold
Same word in English and German. Neuter, like most metals.\nMorgenstund hat Gold im Mund = the early bird catches the worm.

A: Is Leipzig the golden city?
B: No, but there are golden moments here.
A: Like what?
B: Sunset at the market square. Pure golden hour.

Goldene Zeiten (golden times) is an idiom. Morgenstund hat Gold im Mund (the morning hour has gold in its mouth) means the early bird catches the worm. Most metals are neuter in German: das Gold, das Silber.
God
From Proto-Germanic guthon. Gott sei Dank = Thank God.\nGenitive: Gottes. Gottesdienst = church service.

A: Do Germans say Thank God?
B: Yes, even people who are not religious.
A: What does Gottesdienst mean?
B: Church service. Literally: God's service.

Gott sei Dank (Thank God) is everyday German, even for atheists. Um Gottes willen! (For God's sake!) expresses shock. Gottesdienst (church service) literally means God's service. The genitive form Gottes appears in many compounds.
the cross
From Latin crux. Das Rote Kreuz = the Red Cross.\nKreuzung = intersection/crossroads.

A: What does the cross in the Nikolaikirche mean?
B: It is a Christian symbol. And a symbol of peace.
A: And Kreuzung?
B: A road intersection. Where paths cross.

In Bavaria, a cross hangs in every classroom and courtroom. In the rest of Germany, this is controversial. Kreuzfahrt (cruise) literally means cross-journey, from the Crusades. Das Rote Kreuz (Red Cross) is recognized worldwide.
the uniform
Same word in both languages. Represents both the clothing\nand the conformity it represents.

A: Did the soldiers at the Wall wear uniforms?
B: Yes, always. The uniform showed the power of the state.
A: And the demonstrators?
B: No uniforms. Only candles and courage.
The DDR used Uniformen to show state power. The 1989 protesters faced people in Uniform and stayed peaceful. The contrast between uniformed soldiers and candle-carrying citizens defines that night.
by the way
From übrig (remaining/left over). What remains to be said.\nA casual conversation pivot.

A: By the way, you know what?
B: What?
A: Hildi offered me the Du!
B: Really? That is a big step!

Übrigens is beloved by Germans who change topics mid-sentence. It serves as a casual conversation pivot: whatever was übrig (left over, remaining) to be said. Essential for natural German conversation.
a little while ago, earlier
More specific than früher (earlier in general).\nRefers to something in the recent past, usually the same day.

A: What did you say earlier?
B: That Leipzig is a special city.
A: Earlier in the restaurant?
B: Yes, when we were eating the Leipziger Allerlei.
Vorhin refers to something in the recent past, usually the same day. Was hast du vorhin gemeint? (What did you mean earlier?) is everyday German. More specific than früher (earlier in general).
to influence
Be- + Einfluss (influence) + -en. To apply influence.\nInseparable: always beeinflussen, never einfluss-be.

A: Did Leipzig influence German history?
B: Enormously. The peaceful revolution changed everything.
A: And today?
B: Leipzig influences culture. Music, art, startups.
Die Medien beeinflussen die öffentliche Meinung (The media influence public opinion). Inseparable: always beeinflussen, never broken apart. Every influence is a flowing-in of ideas.
to discover
Ent- (removing) + decken (to cover). To uncover.\nEvery discovery is an uncovering.

A: What did you discover in Leipzig?
B: A lot! The history, the culture, the food.
A: What was the best discovery?
B: Auerbachs Keller. Goethe wrote there!

Ent- + decken (to cover): to uncover. Amerika entdecken (to discover America) and Ich habe ein tolles Restaurant entdeckt (I discovered a great restaurant) use the same logic. Every discovery is an uncovering of something hidden.
Verumius sits in the restaurant and reflects. Hildi said du. He has seen Leipzig. He has felt the Nikolaikirche. Something has changed. Not the grammar. Something inside him.
After des (Genitiv), adjectives always end in -en.
Architektur is feminine Akkusativ. Without article the adjective takes the strong ending -e.
Leipzig has historic architecture from different centuries.
First: adjective ending after während with a plural noun and no article. Second: the unambiguous reciprocal pronoun. Third: adjective for masculine Akkusativ without article.
During difficult times the community helped each other. The citizens showed great courage.
Switching from Sie to du is a milestone in German relationships.
Master all three grammar constructions through intensive practice. Combine adjective declension, genitive forms, and reciprocal verbs in real contexts.
Master all three grammar constructions
Combine two or more constructions per exercise
Show what you have learned about Leipzig and German history
Leipzig has told its story. Now it is Verumius's turn. He has learned the words. He has seen the places. Can he understand the language of the contemporary witnesses?
the drawing
From zeichnen (to draw). Zeichen (sign) + -ung.\nTechnische Zeichnung = technical drawing.

A: What kind of drawing is that?
B: A drawing of the Nikolaikirche. From the 19th century.
A: Who made it?
B: An unknown artist.
The -ung suffix turns verbs into nouns describing the result: zeichnen becomes Zeichnung. Technische Zeichnung (technical drawing) and Bauzeichnung (architectural drawing) are professional terms.
offspring, young talent
Nach (after) + Wuchs (growth). What grows after you.\nAlso a gentle way to ask about children.

A: Does Leipzig have good young talent in music?
B: Yes! Many young musicians study here.
A: And in politics?
B: Also. The young political talent is active.
Nachwuchsförderung (support for young talent) is important in German sports, arts, and industry. Nachwuchs literally means what grows after you: the next generation of talent.
the social worker (male)
Sozial + Arbeiter (worker). Sozialarbeit is a respected\nprofession in Germany.

A: What does a social worker do?
B: He helps people in difficult situations.
A: For example?
B: With integration, with youth problems, in communities.

Sozialarbeit (social work) is a respected profession in Germany. Many Sozialarbeiter help with integration, youth programs, and community building. The profession grew significantly after reunification.
the social worker (female)
The -in suffix marks the feminine form.\nThe profession grew significantly after reunification.

A: Do you work as a social worker?
B: Yes, in a youth project.
A: Do you enjoy the work?
B: Yes, I help young people. That is fulfilling.
In Germany, Sozialarbeiterinnen und Sozialarbeiter work in schools, hospitals, refugee centers, and community organizations. The profession is essential for social cohesion.
here (to this place)
Hier (here) + her (toward the speaker). Directional: motion toward here.\nKomm hierher! = Come here!

A: How did you get here?
B: By ICE. From Berlin to Leipzig.
A: Come here! I will show you something.
B: What?
Hierher is directional: motion toward the speaker. Hier means at this place (no motion). Wie bist du hierher gekommen? (How did you get here?) implies the journey. Contrasts with dorthin (to there).
to come from, to originate
Always about origins, never current residence.\nDas Wort stammt aus dem Lateinischen = The word comes from Latin.

A: Where do you come from originally?
B: I come from Leipzig. But I live in Berlin.
A: And this building?
B: It dates from the 16th century.

Ich stamme aus Leipzig (I come from Leipzig originally) is always about origins, never about current residence. Das Wort stammt aus dem Lateinischen (The word comes from Latin). Stamm means trunk, the root of a tree.
to take over, to adopt
Über (over) + nehmen (to take). Inseparable in this meaning.\nVerantwortung übernehmen = to take on responsibility.

A: Who took on responsibility after the Wende?
B: Many civil rights activists.
A: Did they take on political offices?
B: Yes, some became politicians.
Verantwortung übernehmen (to take on responsibility) is a key phrase in German civic life. Die Firma wurde übernommen (The company was taken over) uses the business meaning.
to check, to verify
Über (over) + prüfen (to test). Testing thoroughly.\nMore thorough than prüfen alone.

A: Have you verified the facts?
B: Yes, everything is correct.
A: Good. One must always verify.
B: Especially with historical topics.
Die Fakten überprüfen (to verify the facts) is essential in journalism and history. Inseparable: the über- adds completeness and thoroughness to the basic prüfen (to test).
to publish
Ver- + öffentlich (public) + -en. To make public.\nInseparable. Every publication is a making-public.

A: Was the story of Leipzig published?
B: Yes, in many books and documentaries.
A: Who published about it?
B: Historians, contemporary witnesses, journalists.
Ein Buch veröffentlichen (to publish a book) literally means to make a book public. Die Ergebnisse wurden veröffentlicht (The results were published). Inseparable and formal.
to disappear
Ver- + schwinden (to dwindle). Irregular: verschwindet, verschwand,\nist verschwunden. Spurlos verschwunden = vanished without a trace.

A: Has the Wall completely disappeared?
B: Almost. Only small parts still stand.
A: Where did it disappear to?
B: Torn down. Pieces as souvenirs around the world.

Spurlos verschwunden (vanished without a trace) was a feared phrase in the DDR. People sometimes verschwanden if they opposed the state. Today, die Mauer ist fast verschwunden (the Wall has almost disappeared) is a statement of progress.
Trotz + Genitiv (with article) = -en. Heißer Kaffee = masculine Akkusativ without article = -en.
'Sich' is ambiguous and could mean themselves. Which pronoun makes the reciprocal meaning unmistakably clear?
The citizens from East and West had to learn to trust each other.
Fortschritt is masculine Nominativ. Without article: strong ending -er (like der).
First: article in Genitiv Plural after während. Second and third: the unambiguous reciprocal pronoun used twice. Fourth: adjective after ohne with a feminine noun and no article.
During the assemblies the citizens showed each other great courage. They helped each other without great fear.
After eines (Genitiv), adjectives ALWAYS end in -en.
Stolz is masculine. Dativ without article takes -em (like dem).
They spoke with great pride about their city.
The preposition combines with the reciprocal pronoun to form a single compound word. Which option writes them as one?
different, varying
Unter (between) + schiedlich (separated).\nSynonym: verschieden. Antonym: gleich.

A: The experiences in East and West are different.
B: Yes, the generations had different lives.
A: But they learn from each other.
B: That is important.
From unterscheiden (to distinguish). Things that can be told apart are unterschiedlich. Unterschiedliche Meinungen (different opinions) are valued in German Diskussionskultur.
First: adjective ending after wegen with a feminine noun and no article. Second: the unambiguous reciprocal pronoun. Third: the compound meaning 'with each other.'
Because of a different past, East and West sometimes do not understand each other. Nevertheless they work together.
Check two adjective endings: one after trotz with a neuter noun and no article, one for masculine Akkusativ with no article.
Which word means society as a whole structure, not just a local community?
The peace prayers in the Nikolaikirche completely changed society.
I understand now why Mia is so proud of Leipzig. And I understand why Hildi cried. The past lives in the words. Every word carries a memory.
Wrap up the Leipzig visit with culture and food. Learn the final vocabulary. Meet the Saxon dialect. Reflect on what Leipzig teaches about German identity.
Learn the final 14 vocabulary words of the unit
Discover Saxon dialect and regional culture
Reflect on Leipzig's significance for German identity
The last evening in Leipzig. Mia has chosen a restaurant in the Südvorstadt. Small tables, candles, Saxon cuisine on the menu. She sits down and takes a deep breath. This is where she is home.
Leipziger Allerlei! The traditional dish. It used to be a vegetable stew for rich people. Today everyone eats it. By the way: do not order in Saxon. The waiter will laugh.
Mia talks to the waiter. Her Saxon comes back like an old friend. Verumius understands: Leipzig is not just history. It is Mia's home, the place she comes from. And she is sharing it with him.
the change, the alteration
From ändern (to change) + -ung. More concrete than Veränderung.\nAn Änderung is a specific adjustment, not a deep transformation.

A: Are there changes in the schedule?
B: Yes, starting next week.
A: What kind of changes?
B: New departure times for the ICE to Berlin.
Änderung der Öffnungszeiten (change of opening hours) is a common sign in German shops. More concrete than Veränderung (transformation). An Änderung is a specific, deliberate adjustment.
the development (variant)
Older spelling variant of Entwicklung. Both forms are recognized.\nYou may encounter this in historical texts.

A: Why does it say Entwickelung with an e here?
B: That is an older spelling.
A: Is that wrong?
B: No, just old-fashioned. Today we write Entwicklung.
Entwickelung is an older spelling variant of Entwicklung. The -e- in the middle reflects an earlier pronunciation pattern. You may encounter it in historical texts or older academic writing.
peace
The more common variant of Friede. In Frieden leben = to live in peace.\nFriedensvertrag = peace treaty.

A: Do we live in peace?
B: In Germany yes. For over 80 years.
A: Is peace taken for granted?
B: No. Leipzig shows that you have to fight for it.

Frieden is the more common variant of Friede. In Frieden leben (to live in peace) is what the 1989 demonstrators fought for. Friedensnobelpreis (Nobel Peace Prize) and Friedensvertrag (peace treaty) use this form.
the circle, the district
Im Kreis stehen = to stand in a circle. Landkreis = rural district.\nFreundeskreis = circle of friends.

A: What is a Landkreis?
B: An administrative district. A region.
A: And a Freundeskreis?
B: The group of friends that you have.

From Latin circus via Old High German. One shape, many meanings. Im Kreis stehen (to stand in a circle) is literal. Landkreis (rural district) is administrative. Freundeskreis (circle of friends) is social.
to introduce, to import
Ein (in) + führen (to lead). Leading something in. Separable.\nEr führt ein neues Gesetz ein.

A: When was the D-Mark introduced in the East?
B: In July 1990.
A: And the Euro?
B: 2002. It was introduced across all of Europe.

Ein neues System einführen (to introduce a new system) and Waren einführen (to import goods) use the same verb. Separable: Er führt ein neues Gesetz ein. The Einführung der D-Mark changed East German lives overnight.
to found, to establish
From Grund (ground/reason). To give something ground to stand on.\nEine Firma gründen = to found a company.

A: When was the University of Leipzig founded?
B: 1409. One of the oldest in Germany.
A: And the Gewandhaus Orchestra?
B: Founded in 1743. One of the oldest in the world.

Leipzig's university was gegründet in 1409 and is one of the oldest in Germany. Eine Firma gründen (to found a company) uses the same verb. Die Gründer (the founders) are celebrated in German startup culture.
to represent
Ver- + treten (to step). To step in for someone. Inseparable.\nIch vertrete die Meinung, dass... = I hold the view that...

A: Who represents the interests of the citizens?
B: The elected delegates.
A: Do you hold a point of view?
B: Yes: Leipzig deserves more recognition.
Die Interessen der Bürger vertreten (to represent citizens' interests) is the core function of parliament. Also: sich die Beine vertreten (to stretch one's legs) uses the same verb differently.
on the other hand (contracted)
Contracted form of andererseits. More common in spoken German.\nBoth spellings are correct.

A: On one hand the Leipziger Allerlei is traditional.
B: On the other hand the recipe has changed.
A: Is anderseits correct?
B: Yes, just like andererseits.
Anderseits is a contracted form of andererseits. More common in spoken German. Academic writing prefers andererseits. All three forms (andererseits, anderseits, andrerseits) are correct.
on the other hand (contracted variant)
Another contracted variant of andererseits. Regional preference.\nAll three forms are grammatically correct and interchangeable.

A: On the other hand, one must also see the other side.
B: True. There are always two sides.
A: On one hand... on the other hand...
B: Exactly. That is how you argue in German.
Andrerseits is another contracted variant of andererseits. Regional preference determines which form people use. All three (andererseits, anderseits, andrerseits) are interchangeable.
to here (regional/archaic)
Regional/archaic variant of hierher. Common in Austrian German.\nRecognizing variants helps you understand real spoken German.

A: Come here! Look at this!
B: Is hieher a dialect word?
A: Yes, the Austrian variant of hierher.
B: Ah, good to know!

Hieher is a regional variant of hierher, common in Austrian German and older texts. Komm hieher! = Komm hierher! Standard German uses hierher. Recognizing variants helps you understand real spoken German.
main, chief (prefix)
A prefix that makes any word more important.\nHauptstadt (capital), Hauptbahnhof (main station), Hauptsache (main thing).

A: What is the capital of Saxony?
B: Dresden. But Leipzig is the larger city.
A: And the main thing?
B: The main thing is that you have seen Leipzig!
Haupt- appears in hundreds of compounds. Hauptstadt (capital city), Hauptbahnhof (main train station), Hauptsache (main thing). Leipzig Hauptbahnhof was the starting point of this unit.
under, sub- (prefix)
A prefix meaning below, beneath, or subordinate.\nUnterschied (difference), Unterstützung (support), Unterricht (instruction).

A: What does Unterschied mean?
B: Difference. Unter + Schied, from scheiden (to separate).
A: And Unterricht?
B: Education, teaching. Unter + richten (to direct).

Unterschied (difference) literally means under-separation. Unterstützung (support) means under-propping. Unterricht (instruction) means under-direction. Unter- is one of German's most productive prefixes.
Next week the environmental project starts at the office. You should join in too, Verumius. On one hand it is a lot of work. On the other hand we can change something.
All three come from anderer Seite (other side), just with different contractions.
Which prefix means main or chief?
Leipzig main train station is one of the largest train stations in Europe.
Freiheit is feminine Akkusativ. Without article: strong ending -e.
First: adjective after einer (feminine Genitiv). Second: the unambiguous reciprocal pronoun. Third: adjective after trotz with a masculine noun and no article.
The citizens of a large city helped each other in the fight for freedom. Despite enormous pressure from the government they stayed peaceful.
Mia explained it was once for rich people; today everyone eats it.
enormous, tremendous
From Latin enormis (out of the norm).\nEnormer Einfluss = enormous influence.

A: Leipzig had an enormous influence on music.
B: Yes, Bach, Mendelssohn, Schumann.
A: The influence was enormous.
B: And on politics too.
From Latin enormis (out of the norm). Something enorm is literally beyond the normal scale. Germans use it frequently in both formal and informal contexts.

The ICE back to Berlin. Verumius looks out the window. Leipzig grows smaller. He thinks of the palm columns, of Hildi's photo album, of Werner's face in the old photo. You can go ahead and say du. He smiles. Some words change everything.
